mnemonics: Grow Before You Shrink (Phase 1: Acquiring locks, Phase 2: Releasing locks)
Lock, Work, Unlock (The two distinct phases of a transaction's life)
keyPoints: A transaction must obtain all its locks before it can release any locks, dividing its life into a growing (acquiring) phase and a shrinking (releasing) phase.
Two-phase locking guarantees serializability, ensuring schedules are conflict serializable and therefore correct.
Strict Two-Phase Locking is a common variant where a transaction holds all its exclusive (write) locks until it commits or aborts, which ensures recoverability and avoids cascading rollbacks.
commonMistakes: Confusing two-phase locking with two-phase commit (2PC), which is a distributed database protocol for atomic commitment.
Assuming two-phase locking prevents deadlocks; it does not. Deadlocks can still occur and must be handled by timeouts or deadlock detection algorithms.
Releasing a read lock before acquiring a write lock on the same data item, which can violate the two-phase rule and lead to non-serializable schedules if not done carefully within the growing phase.
